2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.3 Vari: Some types of MQTT Control Packets contain a variable header component.
The variable header resides between the fixed header and the payload.
The Packet Identifier field of variable header is common in several packet types.
2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.1 Stru: The MQTT Control Packet consists of a fixed header, which is present in all MQTT Control Packets, a variable header that is present in some MQTT Control Packets, and a payload that is present in some MQTT Control Packets.

3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.1 CONNECT Гў: The variable header for the CONNECT Packet consists of four fields in the following order: Protocol Name, Protocol Level, Connect Flags, and Keep Alive.
2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.4 Payl: Some MQTT Control Packets contain a payload as the final part of the packet, as described in Chapter 3.
In the case of the PUBLISH packet, the payload is the Application Message.
Table 2.6 - Control Packets that contain a Payload lists the Control Packets that require a Payload.

The table lists MQTT control packets and their payload requirements: CONNECT requires a payload, CONNACK has none, PUBLISH optionally has a payload, PUBACK has none, PUBREC has none, PUBREL has none, PUBCOMP has none, SUBSCRIBE requires a payload, SUBACK requires a payload, UNSUBSCRIBE requires a payload, UNSUBACK has none, PINGREQ has none, PINGRESP has none, and DISCONNECT has none.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.1 CONNECT Гў: The Connect Flags byte also indicates the presence or absence of fields in the payload.

After a Network Connection is established by a Client to a Server, the first Packet sent from the Client to the Server MUST be a CONNECT Packet [MQTT-3.1.0-1].
The Server MUST process a second CONNECT Packet sent from a Client as a protocol violation and disconnect the Client [MQTT-3.1.0-2].
The encoded fields specify a unique Client identifier for the Client, a Will topic, Will Message, User Name and Password.
All but the Client identifier are optional and the presence of these optional fields is determined based on flags in the variable header.

3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.1 CONNECT Гў: The Protocol Level byte is an 8-bit unsigned value representing the revision level of the protocol used by the Client.
The value of the Protocol Level field for the version 3.1.1 of the protocol is 4 (0x04).

The Server MUST respond to the CONNECT Packet with a CONNACK return code 0x01 (unacceptable protocol level) and then disconnect the Client if the Protocol Level is not supported by the Server [MQTT-3.1.2-2].
The Server MUST validate that the reserved flag in the CONNECT Control Packet is set to zero and disconnect the Client if the reserved flag is not zero [MQTT-3.1.2-3].
2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.2 Fixe: The Remaining Length field starts at byte 2.
The Remaining Length is the number of bytes remaining within the current packet, including data in the variable header and the payload.
The maximum number of bytes in the Remaining Length field is four.
CONNACK has value 2, flows from server to client, and is a connect acknowledgment.
UNSUBACK has value 11, flows from server to client, and is an unsubscribe acknowledgment.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.1 CONNECT Гў: The Will QoS is located at bits 4 and 3 of the Connect Flags.
If the Will Flag is set to 0, then the Will QoS MUST be set to 0 (0x00) [MQTT-3.1.2-13].
If the Will Flag is set to 1, the value of Will QoS can be 0 (0x00), 1 (0x01), or 2 (0x02).
The Will QoS value MUST NOT be 3 (0x03) [MQTT-3.1.2-14].
2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.2 Fixe: The table shows the fixed header flags for MQTT control packets.
For the CONNACK packet, bits 3 through 0 are reserved and set to 0.
For the PUBLISH packet in MQTT 3.1.1, bit 3 is the DUP flag, bit 2 is the QoS flag, bit 1 is the QoS flag, and bit 0 is the RETAIN flag.
For the UNSUBSCRIBE packet, bits 3 through 0 are reserved, with bit 1 set to 1 and others to 0.
For the UNSUBACK packet, bits 3 through 0 are reserved and set to 0.
For the DISCONNECT packet, bits 3 through 0 are reserved and set to 0.
DUP1 indicates duplicate delivery of a PUBLISH control packet.
QoS2 refers to the PUBLISH quality of service.
RETAIN3 is the PUBLISH retain flag.
See Section 3.3.1 for a description of the DUP, QoS, and RETAIN flags in the PUBLISH control packet.

The table shows the size of the Remaining Length field: 1 digit from 0 (0x00) to 127 (0x7F), 2 digits from 128 (0x80, 0x01) to 16,383 (0xFF, 0x7F), 3 digits from 16,384 (0x80, 0x80, 0x01) to 2,097,151 (0xFF, 0xFF, 0x7F), and 4 digits from 2,097,152 (0x80, 0x80, 0x80, 0x01) to 268,435,455 (0xFF, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0x7F).

A non-normative comment describes the algorithm for decoding the Remaining Length field: multiplier = 1; value = 0; do encodedByte = next byte from stream; value += (encodedByte AND 127) * multiplier; multiplier *= 128; if (multiplier > 128*128*128) throw Error(Malformed Remaining Length); while ((encodedByte AND 128) != 0); where AND is the bit-wise AND operator (& in C).
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.1 CONNECT Гў: The User Name Flag is located at bit 7 of the Connect Flags.
If the User Name Flag is set to 1, a user name MUST be present in the payload [MQTT-3.1.2-19].
The Password Flag is located at bit 6 of the Connect Flags byte.
If the Password Flag is set to 1, a password MUST be present in the payload [MQTT-3.1.2-21].
If the User Name Flag is set to 0, the Password Flag MUST be set to 0 [MQTT-3.1.2-22].
2 MQTT Control Packet format -> 2.3 Vari: The variable header component of many of the Control Packet types includes a 2-byte Packet Identifier field.
These Control Packets are PUBLISH (where QoS > 0), PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, UNSUBACK.
SUBSCRIBE, UNSUBSCRIBE, and PUBLISH (in cases where QoS > 0) Control Packets MUST contain a non-zero 16-bit Packet Identifier [MQTT-2.3.1-1].
Each time a Client sends a new packet of one of these types, the Client MUST assign the packet a currently unused Packet Identifier [MQTT-2.3.1-2].
If a Client re-sends a particular Control Packet, then the Client MUST use the same Packet Identifier in subsequent re-sends of that packet.
A PUBLISH Packet MUST NOT contain a Packet Identifier if its QoS value is set to 0 [MQTT-2.3.1-5].
A PUBACK, PUBREC or PUBREL Packet MUST contain the same Packet Identifier as the PUBLISH Packet that was originally sent [MQTT-2.3.1-6].
Similarly SUBACK and UNSUBACK MUST contain the Packet Identifier that was used in the corresponding SUBSCRIBE and UNSUBSCRIBE Packet respectively [MQTT-2.3.1-7].
Control Packets that require a Packet Identifier are listed in Table 2.5 - Control Packets that contain a Packet Identifier.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.1 CONNECT Гў: The Will Retain Flag is located at bit 5 of the Connect Flags.
If the Will Flag is set to 0, then the Will Retain Flag MUST be set to 0 [MQTT-3.1.2-15].
If the Will Flag is set to 1 and the Will Retain Flag is set to 0, the Server MUST publish the Will Message as a non-retained message [MQTT-3.1.2-16].
If the Will Flag is set to 1 and the Will Retain Flag is set to 1, the Server MUST publish the Will Message as a retained message [MQTT-3.1.2-17].
The payload of the CONNECT Packet contains one or more length-prefixed fields, whose presence is determined by the flags in the variable header.
These fields, if present, MUST appear in the order Client Identifier, Will Topic, Will Message, User Name, Password [MQTT-3.1.3-1].
If the Will Flag is set to 1, the Will Topic is the next field in the payload.
The Will Topic MUST be a UTF-8 encoded string as defined in Section 1.5.3 [MQTT-3.1.3-10].

If the User Name Flag is set to 1, the User Name is the next field in the payload.
The User Name MUST be a UTF-8 encoded string as defined in Section 1.5.3 [MQTT-3.1.3-11].

3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.1 CONNECT Гў: If the Will Flag is set to 1 the Will Message is the next field in the payload.
The Will Message defines the Application Message that is to be published to the Will Topic as described in Section 3.1.2.5.
The Will Message field consists of a two-byte length followed by the payload for the Will Message expressed as a sequence of zero or more bytes.
When the Will Message is published to the Will Topic its payload consists only of the data portion of this field, not the first two length bytes.

If the Will Flag is set to 1, the Will QoS and Will Retain fields in the Connect Flags will be used by the Server, and the Will Topic and Will Message fields MUST be present in the payload [MQTT-3.1.2-9].
The Will Message MUST be removed from the stored Session state in the Server once the Will Message has been published or the Server has received a DISCONNECT packet from the Client [MQTT-3.1.2-10].

If the Will Flag is set to 0 the Will QoS and Will Retain fields in the Connect Flags MUST be set to zero and the Will Topic and Will Message fields MUST NOT be present in the payload [MQTT-3.1.2-11].

The Server SHOULD publish Will Messages promptly.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.2 CONNACK Гў: The CONNACK Packet is the packet sent by the Server in response to a CONNECT Packet received from a Client.
The first packet sent from the Server to the Client MUST be a CONNACK Packet [MQTT-3.2.0-1].
If the Client does not receive a CONNACK Packet from the Server within a reasonable amount of time, the Client SHOULD close the Network Connection.

The CONNACK Packet has no payload.
Bit 0 is the Session Present Flag.

The CONNACK packet variable header consists of two bytes: Byte 1 contains Connect Acknowledge Flags with bits 7-1 reserved (set to 0) and bit 0 marked as X, while Byte 2 contains the Connect Return code with all bits marked as X.

3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.2 CONNACK Гў: If the Server accepts a connection with CleanSession set to 1, the Server MUST set Session Present to 0 in the CONNACK packet in addition to setting a zero return code in the CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.2.2-1].
If the Server accepts a connection with CleanSession set to 0, the value set in Session Present depends on whether the Server already has stored Session state for the supplied client ID.
If the Server has stored Session state, it MUST set Session Present to 1 in the CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.2.2-2].
If the Server does not have stored Session state, it MUST set Session Present to 0 in the CONNACK packet.
This is in addition to setting a zero return code in the CONNACK packet [MQTT-3.2.2-3].
If a server sends a CONNACK packet containing a non-zero return code, the server MUST set Session Present to 0 [MQTT-3.2.2-4].
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.3 PUBLISH Гў: The Packet Identifier field is only present in PUBLISH Packets where the QoS level is 1 or 2.
Section 2.3.1 provides more information about Packet Identifiers.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.2 CONNACK Гў: Value 2 corresponds to 0x02 Connection Refused, identifier rejected, meaning the Client identifier is correct UTF-8 but not allowed by the Server.
If none of the return codes listed in Table 3.1 вЂ“ Connect Return code values are deemed applicable, then the Server MUST close the Network Connection without sending a CONNACK [MQTT-3.2.2-6].
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.3 PUBLISH Гў: The table shows two fields with their values: Topic Name is 'a/b' and Packet Identifier is '10'.
The QoS definitions are as follows: QoS value 0 corresponds to bits 0 and 0, meaning at most once delivery.
QoS value 1 corresponds to bits 0 and 1, meaning at least once delivery.
QoS value 2 corresponds to bits 1 and 0, meaning exactly once delivery.
A PUBLISH Packet MUST NOT have both QoS bits set to 1.
If a Server or Client receives a PUBLISH Packet which has both QoS bits set to 1, the Server or Client MUST close the Network Connection [MQTT-3.3.1-4].

If the Server does not receive a CONNECT Packet within a reasonable amount of time after the Network Connection is established, the Server SHOULD close the connection.
The Server MUST validate that the CONNECT Packet conforms to section 3.1 and close the Network Connection without sending a CONNACK if the CONNECT Packet does not conform [MQTT-3.1.4-1].
If any of these checks fail, the Server SHOULD send an appropriate CONNACK response with a non-zero return code as described in section 3.2 and the Server MUST close the Network Connection.
If the ClientId represents a Client already connected to the Server then the Server MUST disconnect the existing Client [MQTT-3.1.4-2].
The Server MUST perform the processing of CleanSession that is described in section 3.1.2.4 [MQTT-3.1.4-3].
The Server MUST acknowledge the CONNECT Packet with a CONNACK Packet containing a zero return code [MQTT-3.1.4-4].
Clients are allowed to send further Control Packets immediately after sending a CONNECT Packet; Clients need not wait for a CONNACK Packet to arrive from the Server.
If the Server rejects the CONNECT Packet, the Server MUST NOT process any data sent by the Client after the CONNECT Packet [MQTT-3.1.4-5].
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.3 PUBLISH Гў: The receiver of a PUBLISH Packet MUST respond according to Table 3.4 - Expected Publish Packet response as determined by the QoS in the PUBLISH Packet [MQTT-3.3.4-1].
The Topic Name identifies the information channel to which payload data is published.
The Topic Name MUST be present as the first field in the PUBLISH Packet Variable header.
The Topic Name MUST be a UTF-8 encoded string [MQTT-3.3.2-1] as defined in section 1.5.3.
The Topic Name in the PUBLISH Packet MUST NOT contain wildcard characters [MQTT-3.3.2-2].
The Topic Name in a PUBLISH Packet sent by a Server to a subscribing Client MUST match the SubscriptionвЂ™s Topic Filter according to the matching process defined in Section 4.7 [MQTT-3.3.2-3].
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.4 PUBACK ГўВ: The variable header contains the Packet Identifier from the PUBLISH Packet that is being acknowledged.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.3 PUBLISH Гў: The Payload contains the Application Message that is being published.
The length of the payload can be calculated by subtracting the length of the variable header from the Remaining Length field that is in the Fixed Header.
The table shows expected responses for different QoS levels: For QoS level 0, no response is expected; for QoS level 1, a PUBACK packet is expected; for QoS level 2, a PUBREC packet is expected.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.5 PUBREC ГўВ: The variable header contains the Packet Identifier from the PUBLISH Packet that is being acknowledged.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.4 PUBACK ГўВ: The table shows the fixed header structure for the PUBACK packet.
Byte 1 contains the MQTT Control Packet type value 4 and reserved bits.
Byte 2 contains the Remaining Length field value 2.
The Remaining Length field indicates the length of the variable header.
For the PUBACK Packet, the Remaining Length field has the value 2.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.6 PUBREL ГўВ: The PUBREL Packet is the third packet of the QoS 2 protocol exchange.
The variable header contains the same Packet Identifier as the PUBREC Packet that is being acknowledged.
The PUBREL Packet has no payload.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.3 PUBLISH Гў: The DUP flag is located at byte 1, bit 3.
If the DUP flag is set to 0, it indicates that this is the first occasion that the Client or Server has attempted to send this MQTT PUBLISH Packet.
If the DUP flag is set to 1, it indicates that this might be re-delivery of an earlier attempt to send the Packet.
The DUP flag MUST be set to 1 by the Client or Server when the Client or Server attempts to re-deliver a PUBLISH Packet [MQTT-3.3.1.-1].
The DUP flag MUST be set to 0 for all QoS 0 messages [MQTT-3.3.1-2].
The value of the DUP flag from an incoming PUBLISH packet is not propagated when the PUBLISH Packet is sent to subscribers by the Server.

The DUP flag in the outgoing PUBLISH packet is set independently of the incoming PUBLISH packet; its value MUST be determined solely by whether the outgoing PUBLISH packet is a retransmission [MQTT-3.3.1-3].
**Non normative comment** The recipient of a Control Packet that contains the DUP flag set to 1 cannot assume that the recipient has seen an earlier copy of this packet.
**Non normative comment** It is important to note that the DUP flag refers to the Control Packet itself and not to the Application Message that the Control Packet contains.

When using QoS 1, it is possible for a Client to receive a PUBLISH Packet with DUP flag set to 0 that contains a repetition of an Application Message that the Client received earlier, but with a different Packet Identifier.
Section 2.3.1 provides more information about Packet Identifiers.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.6 PUBREL ГўВ: The table shows bits 7 through 0, with byte 1 containing the Packet Identifier Most Significant Byte and byte 2 containing the Packet Identifier Least Significant Byte.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.7 PUBCOMP Гў: The variable header contains the same Packet Identifier as the PUBREL Packet that is being acknowledged.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.3 PUBLISH Гў: When Clients make subscriptions with Topic Filters that include wildcards, it is possible for a ClientвЂ™s subscriptions to overlap so that a published message might match multiple filters.
In this case the Server MUST deliver the message to the Client respecting the maximum QoS of all the matching subscriptions [MQTT-3.3.5-1].
The Server MUST either make a positive acknowledgement, according to the normal QoS rules, or close the Network Connection [MQTT-3.3.5-2].
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.5 PUBREC ГўВ: The fixed header of the PUBREC packet includes: Bits 7 through 0 in byte 1 show the MQTT Control Packet type (5) and reserved bits with values 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0.
Byte 2 shows the Remaining Length field (2) with values 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0.
The Remaining Length field indicates the length of the variable header.
For the PUBREC packet, the Remaining Length field has the value 2.

3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.3 PUBLISH Гў: The RETAIN flag is located at byte 1, bit 0.

If the RETAIN flag is set to 1 in a PUBLISH Packet sent by a Client to a Server, the Server MUST store the Application Message and its QoS, so that the message can be delivered to future subscribers whose subscriptions match the topic name [MQTT-3.3.1-5].
If the Server receives a QoS 0 message with the RETAIN flag set to 1 it MUST discard any message previously retained for that topic.

The Server SHOULD store the new QoS 0 message as the new retained message for that topic, but MAY choose to discard it at any time - if the Server discards it, there will be no retained message for that topic [MQTT-3.3.1-7].
When sending a PUBLISH Packet to a Client the Server MUST set the RETAIN flag to 1 if a message is sent as a result of a new subscription being made by a Client [MQTT-3.3.1-8].

The Server MUST set the RETAIN flag to 0 when a PUBLISH Packet is sent to a Client because the Client matches an established subscription, regardless of how the flag was set in the message the Server received [MQTT-3.3.1-9].
A PUBLISH Packet with a RETAIN flag set to 1 and a payload containing zero bytes will be processed as normal by the Server and sent to Clients with a subscription matching the topic name.
вЂњAs normalвЂќ means that the RETAIN flag is not set in the message received by existing Clients.
If the RETAIN flag is 0, in a PUBLISH Packet sent by a Client to a Server, the Server MUST NOT store the message and MUST NOT remove or replace any existing retained message [MQTT-3.3.1-12].
**Remaining Length field** This is the length of variable header plus the length of the payload.

3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.9 SUBACK ГўВ: A SUBACK Packet is sent by the Server to the Client to confirm receipt and processing of a SUBSCRIBE Packet.
A SUBACK Packet contains a list of return codes that specify the maximum QoS level granted for each subscription requested in the SUBSCRIBE Packet.
The variable header contains the Packet Identifier from the SUBSCRIBE Packet that is being acknowledged.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE: The SUBSCRIBE packet fixed header consists of two bytes.
Byte 1 contains the MQTT Control Packet type (8) and reserved bits set to 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 0.
Byte 2 contains the Remaining Length field.
Bits 3, 2, 1, and 0 of the fixed header are reserved and must be set to 0, 0, 1, and 0 respectively.
The Remaining Length field represents the length of the variable header (2 bytes) plus the length of the payload.
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.9 SUBACK ГўВ: Byte 1 contains the MQTT Control Packet type (9) and reserved bits with values 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0.
Byte 2 contains the Remaining Length field.
The Remaining Length field specifies the length of the variable header (2 bytes) plus the length of the payload.
Figure 3.27 - Payload byte format non normative example shows the payload for the SUBACK Packet, which is briefly described in Table 3.6 - Payload non normative example.
The payload contains a list of return codes.
Each return code corresponds to a Topic Filter in the SUBSCRIBE Packet being acknowledged.
The order of return codes in the SUBACK Packet MUST match the order of Topic Filters in the SUBSCRIBE Packet [MQTT-3.9.3-1].
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.8 SUBSCRIBE: The payload of a SUBSCRIBE Packet contains a list of Topic Filters indicating the Topics to which the Client wants to subscribe.
The Topic Filters in a SUBSCRIBE packet payload MUST be UTF-8 encoded strings as defined in Section 1.5.3 [MQTT-3.8.3-1].
Each filter is followed by a byte called the Requested QoS.
The Requested QoS gives the maximum QoS level at which the Server can send Application Messages to the Client.
The payload of a SUBSCRIBE packet MUST contain at least one Topic Filter / QoS pair.
A SUBSCRIBE packet with no payload is a protocol violation [MQTT-3.8.3-3].
The requested maximum QoS field is encoded in the byte following each UTF-8 encoded topic name, and these Topic Filter / QoS pairs are packed contiguously.

The SUBSCRIBE packet payload format includes: Description columns for bits 7 through 0, Topic Filter byte 1, Length MSB byte 2, Length LSB bytes 3 through N, Topic Filter Requested QoS, Reserved, and QoS byte N+1 with bits set to 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, X, X.
The upper 6 bits of the Requested QoS byte are not used in the current version of the protocol.
The upper 6 bits of the Requested QoS byte are reserved for future use.
The Server MUST treat a SUBSCRIBE packet as malformed and close the Network Connection if any of the Reserved bits in the payload are non-zero, or the QoS value is not 0, 1, or 2 [MQTT-3-8.3-4].
3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.10 UNSUBSCRI: The variable header contains a Packet Identifier.
Section 2.3.1 provides more information about Packet Identifiers.

An UNSUBSCRIBE packet with no payload is a protocol violation [MQTT-3.10.3-2].

The Server MUST complete the delivery of any QoS 1 or QoS 2 messages which the Server has started to send to the Client [MQTT-3.10.4-3].
The Server MUST respond to an UNSUBSCRIBE request by sending an UNSUBACK packet.
The UNSUBACK Packet MUST have the same Packet Identifier as the UNSUBSCRIBE Packet [MQTT-3.10.4-4].

3 MQTT Control Packets -> 3.14 DISCONNEC: After sending a DISCONNECT Packet, the Client must close the Network Connection.
The Client must not send any more Control Packets on that Network Connection.
On receipt of a DISCONNECT Packet, the Server must discard any Will Message associated with the current connection without publishing it, as described in Section 3.1.2.5.
4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of: MQTT delivers Application Messages according to the Quality of Service (QoS) levels defined here.
The QoS level used to deliver an Application Message outbound to the Client could differ from the QoS level of the inbound Application Message.
In the QoS 0 delivery protocol, the Sender MUST send a PUBLISH packet with QoS=0 and DUP=0 [MQTT-4.3.1-1].
In the QoS 0 delivery protocol, the Receiver accepts ownership of the message when the Receiver receives the PUBLISH packet.
4 Operational behavior -> 4.4 Message de: When a Client reconnects with CleanSession set to 0, both the Client and Server MUST re-send any unacknowledged PUBLISH Packets (where QoS > 0) and PUBREL Packets using their original Packet Identifiers [MQTT-4.4.0-1].
4 Operational behavior -> 4.3 Quality of: The sender stores the message and sends a PUBLISH packet with QoS 1 and DUP 0.
The receiver initiates onward delivery of the application message and sends a PUBACK packet with the packet identifier.
When the original sender receives the PUBACK packet, ownership of the application message is transferred to the receiver.

The Client MUST acknowledge any Publish Packet the Client receives according to the applicable QoS rules regardless of whether the Client elects to process the Application Message that the Publish Packet contains [MQTT-4.5.0-2].

The PUBREC packet with Packet Identifier is sent back.
The PUBREL packet with Packet Identifier is sent.
When the original sender receives the PUBREC packet, ownership of the Application Message is transferred to the receiver.
